content.text: CI Eval Gates and Quality Bars

An eval gate is a CI check that runs the eval suite against a baseline and fails the build when a quality bar is breached. Like a failing unit test, a failing eval gate blocks a PR from merging until the regression is either fixed or the baseline is intentionally updated. Unlike a unit test, an eval gate produces probabilistic results — the same prompt can receive slightly different scores across runs — so the gate logic must account for variance rather than asserting exact equality.

The quality bar is the set of thresholds that separate acceptable model behaviour from a regression. Setting the bar too tight (any change in pass rate blocks) makes the gate flaky and developers learn to bypass it. Setting it too loose (only catastrophic failures block) means the gate catches nothing until production. Calibrate the quality bar to the realistic variance of your eval suite, then tighten it as the suite matures.

This sub-subtopic covers the three-level gate decision flow (hard regression → suite pass rate → latency), how to encode quality bars in configuration rather than hardcoded constants, the CI workflow structure that runs evals efficiently, and how to handle gate flakiness without losing the gate's protective value.
Gate Decision Flow

attrs.alt: Eval gate decision flow: hard regression check → suite pass rate check → latency check

content.text: Every eval gate run passes through three checks in order. The checks are ordered by severity and cost of investigation — cheaper checks run first and block earlier if they fail:
Check
What It Tests
Blocking?
Threshold
Hard regression
Any individual case that was PASS in the baseline is now FAIL
Yes — always block
Zero tolerance: any PASS→FAIL is a hard block
Suite pass rate drop
The overall pass rate of the suite has dropped by more than the configured tolerance
Yes — block if delta exceeds tolerance
Configurable; typical starting value: 1% (block if suite drops from 97% to below 96%)
Latency regression
The p99 latency across all eval cases has increased by more than the threshold
No — warn only
Configurable; typical: 20% increase triggers a warning ticket, not a block
Quality Bar Configuration

Quality bars must be configuration, not magic numbers in code. A quality bar embedded in a script cannot be updated without a code review. A quality bar in a YAML file can be updated by anyone with access and creates a clear audit trail of threshold changes:

# evals/quality_bars.yaml
# Quality bars for each eval suite.
# To tighten a bar: lower pass_rate_floor or tolerance.
# To loosen a bar: raise tolerance (requires team review — comment required).
# All changes to this file require a reviewer from @eval-team (see CODEOWNERS).

ticket_classifier:
  pass_rate_floor: 0.95          # Suite must pass at least 95% of cases
  hard_regression_tolerance: 0   # Any PASS→FAIL is a hard block (always 0)
  pass_rate_drop_tolerance: 0.01 # Block if suite pass rate drops > 1%
  latency_warn_pct: 20           # Warn if p99 latency increases > 20%
  latency_block_pct: null        # null = never block on latency alone

summary_agent:
  pass_rate_floor: 0.90
  hard_regression_tolerance: 0
  pass_rate_drop_tolerance: 0.02
  latency_warn_pct: 30
  latency_block_pct: null

refusal_classifier:
  pass_rate_floor: 0.98          # Tighter bar for safety-critical feature
  hard_regression_tolerance: 0
  pass_rate_drop_tolerance: 0.005
  latency_warn_pct: 15
  latency_block_pct: null

# evals/gate.py
"""
Eval gate: load quality bars and evaluate a run result against them.
Called by the CI runner after evals/runner.py produces a result dict.
"""
import sys, yaml
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

_BARS_PATH = Path("evals/quality_bars.yaml")

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class QualityBar:
    pass_rate_floor:           float
    hard_regression_tolerance: int
    pass_rate_drop_tolerance:  float
    latency_warn_pct:          float
    latency_block_pct:         Optional[float]

def load_bar(suite_name: str) -> QualityBar:
    raw = yaml.safe_load(_BARS_PATH.read_text())
    cfg = raw.get(suite_name)
    if cfg is None:
        raise ValueError(f"No quality bar configured for suite '{suite_name}'")
    return QualityBar(
        pass_rate_floor           = cfg["pass_rate_floor"],
        hard_regression_tolerance = cfg["hard_regression_tolerance"],
        pass_rate_drop_tolerance  = cfg["pass_rate_drop_tolerance"],
        latency_warn_pct          = cfg["latency_warn_pct"],
        latency_block_pct         = cfg.get("latency_block_pct"),
    )

def evaluate_gate(suite_name: str, current: dict, baseline: dict) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
    """
    Evaluate the current run against the baseline using the quality bar.
    Returns (gate_passed: bool, messages: list[str]).
    messages contains failure or warning details.
    """
    bar      = load_bar(suite_name)
    messages = []
    gate_ok  = True

    # Check 1: Hard regressions
    current_map  = {r["id"]: r["result"] for r in current["results"]}
    baseline_map = {r["id"]: r["result"] for r in baseline.get("results", [])}
    hard = [
        cid for cid, cur in current_map.items()
        if baseline_map.get(cid) == "PASS" and cur != "PASS"
    ]
    if len(hard) > bar.hard_regression_tolerance:
        gate_ok = False
        for cid in hard:
            messages.append(f"HARD REGRESSION: {cid} was PASS, now {current_map[cid]}")

    # Check 2: Suite pass rate
    current_rate  = current["pass_rate"]
    baseline_rate = baseline.get("pass_rate", 1.0)
    delta         = baseline_rate - current_rate
    if delta > bar.pass_rate_drop_tolerance:
        gate_ok = False
        messages.append(
            f"PASS RATE DROP: {baseline_rate:.2%} → {current_rate:.2%} "
            f"(drop={delta:.2%}, tolerance={bar.pass_rate_drop_tolerance:.2%})"
        )
    if current_rate < bar.pass_rate_floor:
        gate_ok = False
        messages.append(
            f"BELOW PASS RATE FLOOR: {current_rate:.2%} < {bar.pass_rate_floor:.2%}"
        )

    # Check 3: Latency
    cur_lat  = current.get("p99_latency_ms", 0)
    base_lat = baseline.get("p99_latency_ms", 0)
    if base_lat > 0 and cur_lat > 0:
        lat_delta = (cur_lat - base_lat) / base_lat * 100
        if bar.latency_block_pct and lat_delta > bar.latency_block_pct:
            gate_ok = False
            messages.append(f"LATENCY REGRESSION (blocking): p99 +{lat_delta:.1f}%")
        elif lat_delta > bar.latency_warn_pct:
            messages.append(f"LATENCY WARNING (non-blocking): p99 +{lat_delta:.1f}%")

    return gate_ok, messages
CI Workflow with Gate Integration

# .github/workflows/eval-gate.yml
name: Eval gate

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'src/**/*.py'
      - 'tools/**'
      - 'evals/quality_bars.yaml'

jobs:
  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run eval suite
        id: eval
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python -m evals.runner \
            --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json \
            --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json \
            --output eval_results.json

      - name: Evaluate gate
        run: |
          python -m evals.gate \
            --suite ticket_classifier \
            --results eval_results.json \
            --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json

      - name: Upload results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results-${{ github.sha }}
          path: eval_results.json
          retention-days: 30
Handling Gate Flakiness

Eval gates can be flaky when the model's output is near the boundary of a scorer's pass/fail decision. A test that passes 9 out of 10 times is not a reliable gate. Three strategies reduce eval gate flakiness without relaxing the quality bar:
Run each case N times and use the majority result
: instead of running each eval case once, run it 3 times and take the majority (at least 2 out of 3 must PASS). This adds API cost but eliminates single-run variance.
Choose scorers that are binary on clear boundaries

: a scorer that checks for a refusal phrase is deterministic — the phrase is either present or not. A scorer that asks 'is this response helpful?' has a fuzzy boundary and will produce inconsistent results. Prefer binary scorers over gradient ones.
Use temperature=0 in eval runs

: the Claude API does not guarantee determinism at temperature=0, but in practice lower temperature produces more consistent outputs across runs. Set temperature=0 specifically in the eval runner config, not in the production config (which may intentionally use a different temperature).

# evals/runner.py — majority-vote variant for flakiness reduction
import anthropic
from evals.scorers import SCORER_REGISTRY
from src.ticket_classifier import _PARAMS
from dataclasses import replace

_EVAL_PARAMS = replace(_PARAMS, temperature=0.0)   # Override for eval stability

def run_case_majority(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    case: dict,
    n_runs: int = 3,
) -> dict:
    """
    Run a case N times and return PASS if majority pass, FAIL otherwise.
    Reduces flakiness caused by model output variance near scorer boundaries.
    """
    results = []
    for _ in range(n_runs):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=_EVAL_PARAMS.model,
            max_tokens=_EVAL_PARAMS.max_tokens,
            temperature=_EVAL_PARAMS.temperature,
            system=_EVAL_PARAMS.system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        scorer = SCORER_REGISTRY[case["scorer"]]
        results.append(scorer(response))

    passes    = sum(results)
    majority  = passes > n_runs // 2
    return {
        "id":         case["id"],
        "result":     "PASS" if majority else "FAIL",
        "pass_count": passes,
        "run_count":  n_runs,
    }
Reporting Eval Results as PR Comments

A CI eval gate that only shows a green/red status check is hard to act on — the developer must open the CI logs to understand what failed. A structured PR comment that summarises the eval run directly in the pull request makes the gate results immediately actionable:

# scripts/post_eval_comment.py
"""
Post a structured eval gate summary as a PR comment using the GitHub API.
Run after the eval gate step in CI, passing the results JSON and the gate outcome.

Usage:
    python scripts/post_eval_comment.py \
        --results eval_results.json \
        --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json \
        --suite ticket_classifier \
        --gate-passed true|false \
        --pr-number ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
        --repo ${{ github.repository }}
"""
import argparse, json, os, sys
from pathlib import Path

def build_comment(
    suite:       str,
    current:     dict,
    baseline:    dict,
    gate_passed: bool,
    messages:    list[str],
) -> str:
    status_icon = "✅" if gate_passed else "❌"
    lines = [
        f"## {status_icon} Eval Gate: `{suite}`",
        "",
        f"| Metric | Baseline | Current | Delta |",
        f"|--------|----------|---------|-------|",
    ]
    cur_rate  = current["pass_rate"]
    base_rate = baseline.get("pass_rate", 1.0)
    delta     = cur_rate - base_rate
    delta_str = f"+{delta:.2%}" if delta >= 0 else f"{delta:.2%}"
    lines.append(f"| Pass rate | {base_rate:.2%} | {cur_rate:.2%} | {delta_str} |")
    lines.append(f"| Cases run | {baseline.get('total', '—')} | {current['total']} | — |")

    cur_lat  = current.get("p99_latency_ms")
    base_lat = baseline.get("p99_latency_ms")
    if cur_lat and base_lat:
        lat_delta = (cur_lat - base_lat) / base_lat * 100
        lat_str   = f"+{lat_delta:.1f}%" if lat_delta >= 0 else f"{lat_delta:.1f}%"
        lines.append(f"| p99 latency | {base_lat}ms | {cur_lat}ms | {lat_str} |")

    if messages:
        lines += ["", "### Gate messages", ""]
        for msg in messages:
            icon = "🔴" if "REGRESSION" in msg or "DROP" in msg or "FLOOR" in msg else "🟡"
            lines.append(f"- {icon} {msg}")

    # List individual regressions
    cur_map  = {r["id"]: r["result"] for r in current["results"]}
    base_map = {r["id"]: r["result"] for r in baseline.get("results", [])}
    hard = [cid for cid, cr in cur_map.items() if base_map.get(cid) == "PASS" and cr != "PASS"]
    if hard:
        lines += ["", "### Hard regressions (PASS → FAIL)", ""]
        for cid in hard:
            lines.append(f"- `{cid}`")

    lines += ["", f"*Eval run: `{current.get('timestamp', 'unknown')}`*"]
    return "\n".join(lines)

def post_github_comment(repo: str, pr_number: int, body: str) -> None:
    import urllib.request, urllib.parse
    token = os.environ.get("GITHUB_TOKEN")
    if not token:
        print("GITHUB_TOKEN not set — skipping PR comment", file=sys.stderr)
        return
    url  = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/issues/{pr_number}/comments"
    data = json.dumps({"body": body}).encode()
    req  = urllib.request.Request(url, data=data, method="POST")
    req.add_header("Authorization", f"Bearer {token}")
    req.add_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    req.add_header("Accept", "application/vnd.github+json")
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        if resp.status not in (200, 201):
            print(f"Failed to post comment: HTTP {resp.status}", file=sys.stderr)
        else:
            print("PR comment posted successfully.")

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--results",     type=Path, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--baseline",    type=Path, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--suite",       required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--gate-passed", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--pr-number",   type=int)
    parser.add_argument("--repo")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    current     = json.loads(args.results.read_text())
    baseline    = json.loads(args.baseline.read_text())
    gate_passed = args.gate_passed.lower() == "true"

    from evals.gate import evaluate_gate
    _, messages = evaluate_gate(args.suite, current, baseline)
    body = build_comment(args.suite, current, baseline, gate_passed, messages)
    print(body)

    if args.pr_number and args.repo:
        post_github_comment(args.repo, args.pr_number, body)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Add this step to eval-gate.yml after the gate evaluation step:
- name: Post eval summary to PR
  if: always()   # Post even if the gate failed
  env:
    GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
  run: |
    python scripts/post_eval_comment.py \
      --results eval_results.json \
      --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json \
      --suite ticket_classifier \
      --gate-passed ${{ steps.gate.outcome == 'success' && 'true' || 'false' }} \
      --pr-number ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
      --repo ${{ github.repository }}
Updating the Quality Bar
When an intentional prompt change shifts model behaviour, the baseline must be updated and the quality bar may need to be loosened or tightened. Both are config changes that require a code review:
Run the eval suite against the new prompt and inspect which cases changed.
If the changes are expected (new refusal phrases, different JSON field names), update the baseline: python -m evals.runner --update-baseline.
If the new pass rate is lower than the current pass_rate_floor, discuss with the team whether to accept the new baseline as the new normal or to revise the prompt until the quality recovers.
Commit evals/baselines/ and evals/quality_bars.yaml together in the same commit as the prompt change so the CI gate and the baseline agree on the same commit.
If you are loosening a quality bar (raising tolerance), add a comment in quality_bars.yaml explaining why: # Loosened 2025-06-15: new summariser has higher output variance; tighten after 2 weeks.
Summary

CI eval gates run the eval suite against a baseline and block merges when a quality bar is breached. The gate checks three things in order: hard regressions (any PASS→FAIL on individual cases, always blocking), suite pass rate drops (blocking when the delta exceeds a per-suite tolerance), and latency regressions (non-blocking by default, warning only). Quality bars are defined in a YAML file owned by the eval team so threshold changes require a code review and leave an audit trail. Flakiness is reduced by using temperature=0 in eval runs, choosing binary scorers over gradient ones, and optionally using majority-vote evaluation (N runs per case). When a quality bar is loosened to accept a new baseline, the loosening is commented in the config with an expiry date to force a follow-up review.
